macro_events.event_summary: Trump signals he'll let Kevin Warsh — widely seen as the next Fed Chair pick — "do what he wants" on interest rates, raising market expectations of a more dovish, politically-aligned Fed under Warsh.
primary_drivers: Lower expected policy rate path / steeper curve via long-end inflation premium
Erosion of Fed independence premium → weaker USD, higher inflation breakevens

beneficiaries.rationale: Bitcoin-proxy equity with maximum beta to crypto rallies driven by Fed credibility concerns.

losers.rationale: Money-center banks face curve dynamics that are mixed but net negative if long end rises on inflation premium while deposit competition stays intense; also exposed to credibility-driven volatility in fixed income books.

headlines: Trump says he’ll let Warsh ‘do what he wants to do’ with interest rates. It’s a remark that Fed watchers have been bracing for.

macro_events.event_summary: Trump downplays a conflict as "not a war," but insurers underwriting the risk are treating it as one — which has direct implications for war-exclusion clauses, claims liability, and reinsurance pricing.
primary_drivers: War-exclusion clauses in P&C/specialty insurance policies — if classified as 'war,' insurers can deny claims; if not, they pay out
Reinsurance pricing and capacity tightening for political violence / terrorism / marine / aviation lines
Headline uncertainty around insurer claims liability

beneficiaries.rationale: RenaissanceRe is a leading specialty reinsurer; ambiguity over war classification drives demand for political violence/terrorism reinsurance at higher rates, and RNR has historically gained pricing power in hard-market conditions for specialty lines.

Arthur J. Gallagher is a broker — brokers earn commissions on placements regardless of who pays claims. Increased demand for war/political violence coverage and policy rewrites drives brokerage revenue without underwriting risk.

losers.rationale: Chubb is one of the largest writers of specialty/political risk and marine/aviation insurance. If the event is NOT classified as 'war,' war-exclusion clauses don't apply and Chubb is on the hook for claims it would otherwise deny.
AIG has significant specialty lines exposure including political risk, terrorism, and marine cargo — same war-exclusion ambiguity directly affects claim payouts.
Hartford writes commercial specialty lines that could be exposed to disputed war-exclusion claims; secondary exposure relative to CB/AIG.
Loews owns CNA Financial, a commercial P&C insurer with specialty lines exposure to the same war-exclusion dispute mechanism.

headlines: Trump says it's not a 'war.' Insurers with money on the line say it is

macro_events.event_summary: Trump revealed he was within an hour of ordering a strike on Iran before postponing the decision, signaling acute but deferred Middle East military risk.
primary_drivers: Geopolitical risk premium in oil from potential Strait of Hormuz disruption
Defense spending and munitions demand expectations

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US integrated oil major with significant upstream leverage; any Iran strike premium in Brent crude flows directly to E&P earnings. Most liquid vehicle for headline-driven oil risk premium trade.
High-beta US oil producer with concentrated Permian production; historically moves 1.5-2x crude on geopolitical spikes, making it the preferred high-beta crude proxy.

Prime contractor for THAAD, PAC-3 Patriot missiles, and JASSM — directly used/replenished in any Iran strike scenario and across Israeli/Gulf allied missile defense. Standard defense headline beneficiary.
Maker of Tomahawk cruise missiles (primary standoff strike weapon vs Iran), SM-3/SM-6 interceptors, and Patriot systems. Direct munitions consumption exposure in any strike campaign.
B-2 stealth bomber sustainment and long-range strike systems; the platform most associated with deep Iran strike scenarios (fortified nuclear sites).
Cheniere is largest US LNG exporter; Strait of Hormuz disruption fears boost European/Asian demand for non-Gulf LNG and widen TTF-Henry Hub spreads.

losers.rationale: Heaviest international/transpacific and Gulf-routing exposure among US carriers; jet fuel is ~25-30% of opex and Middle East airspace closures force costly reroutes. Most sensitive US airline to crude spike + geopolitical disruption.
Major transatlantic exposure and high jet fuel sensitivity; crude spike compresses near-term margins, no fuel hedging program.
Cruise operator with significant Mediterranean/Middle East itineraries and high bunker fuel exposure; geopolitical risk pressures bookings and unit costs simultaneously.

Retailers with thin margins are squeezed by higher gasoline prices (consumer wallet drain) and diesel-driven logistics costs; Walmart most-watched bellwether for consumer discretionary spend compression.
High-beta, high-multiple name vulnerable to risk-off rotation and rising oil-driven inflation expectations lifting back-end yields; sentiment-driven drawdown vehicle.

headlines: Trump says he was 'an hour away' from Iran strike decision before he postponed it

macro_events.event_summary: Strait of Hormuz disruption is forcing crude shipments onto longer alternative routes, tightening tanker capacity and pressuring global oil logistics.
primary_drivers: Ton-mile demand surge for crude tankers as voyages lengthen
Supply premium on non-Gulf crude grades (US, Brazil, West Africa)
Insurance/war-risk premia and rerouting costs raising delivered crude prices

beneficiaries.rationale: Frontline is a pure-play crude tanker operator (VLCC/Suezmax); longer voyages around Hormuz mechanically increase ton-miles and spot day rates, directly lifting earnings.
International Seaways operates VLCCs and Suezmaxes carrying Middle East crude; rerouting forces longer ballast/laden voyages, tightening fleet utilization and spot rates.
DHT Holdings is a pure-play VLCC owner with high spot exposure — direct leverage to the ton-mile expansion from Hormuz workarounds.
Teekay Tankers (Suezmax/Aframax) benefits as mid-size crude carriers pick up rerouted Atlantic basin and West African barrels replacing Gulf supply into Asia/Europe.
EOG is a pure-play US onshore crude producer; non-Gulf barrels command a premium as buyers scramble for replacement supply, and WTI strength flows directly to realized prices.
Diamondback is a Permian pure-play; Brent-WTI spread widening on Hormuz disruption boosts US export economics and Permian-linked netbacks.
Petrobras exports Atlantic-basin medium/heavy crude that becomes a natural substitute for diverted Gulf barrels into Asian refiners, supporting realized prices and volumes.

losers.rationale: Delta has limited fuel hedging and jet fuel is a top-3 cost line; sustained Brent strength from Hormuz disruption directly compresses unit margins.
United is highly exposed to international long-haul routes that burn the most fuel and has historically not hedged; rising jet crack spreads from crude tightness hit EPS directly.
Southwest's all-737 domestic network has high fuel intensity per ASM and the carrier has unwound most hedges; higher crude flows straight to operating cost.
Dow's ethylene/polyethylene cracker economics are squeezed when naphtha and broader hydrocarbon feedstock costs rise on global crude tightness, particularly at European assets.
LyondellBasell's European crackers run on naphtha which tracks Brent; Hormuz-driven crude premium compresses olefin chain margins.
Marathon Petroleum's US refining complex faces higher crude input costs without proportional product price gains if demand softens, and longer voyage times disrupt sour crude slate optimization.

headlines: Crude oil is going the long way around the world as countries scramble to plug Hormuz supply hole

macro_events.event_summary: The 10-year Treasury yield is rising in a way that's becoming a headwind for equities, pressuring rate-sensitive sectors.
primary_drivers: Higher discount rate compresses long-duration equity valuations
Higher mortgage/borrowing costs hit housing and credit-sensitive consumers
Net interest margin expansion benefits banks/insurers with asset-sensitive books

beneficiaries.rationale: Schwab's earnings are highly asset-sensitive; higher 10Y yields lift reinvestment yields on its securities portfolio and reduce unrealized loss drag, directly improving NIM.
Life insurer with long-duration liabilities — higher 10Y yields improve reinvestment income and lift book value economics on new policy spreads.
Same mechanism as MET — Prudential's general account reinvests at higher yields, expanding spread income on annuity and life products.

losers.rationale: Largest US homebuilder; demand is directly tied to mortgage rates which track the 10Y. Incentive/buydown costs rise as yields climb, compressing margins.
Long-duration high-multiple semi/IP name with valuation extremely sensitive to discount rate — multiple compression risk on rising yields.
Trades at extreme forward multiples; long-duration cash flows get hit hardest by DCF discount rate increases.
Realty Income — REITs are bond proxies with high leverage; rising 10Y raises cap rates and refinancing costs, compressing NAV and dividend spread attractiveness.
Rocket Companies — mortgage origination volumes collapse as 10Y rises and 30Y mortgage rates follow; direct revenue hit.

headlines: The 10-year Treasury yield is moving the 'wrong way' for stocks
